interactions.summary: Jiang explains why trust, bank runs, royal default, and intermarried banking cartels lead from merchant finance toward central banking, then pivots from money creation to the question of poverty.
Students offer scarcity or limited real resources as the reason poverty persists.

speaker_notes.note: Short student interjections are captured by different diarized speakers; wording is partial but interaction role is clear.

claims.claim: Early money could work among merchants because the circle was small enough that mutual recognition and reputation reduced forgery problems.
A bank run exposes the fragility of multiplying receipts beyond the actual gold reserve.
Banking cartels reduce risk by pooling reserves and by coordinating political violence against kings who refuse repayment.
Central banking is introduced as a power system that turns contracts, which are nothing materially, into everything socially.
Once banks can print money, poverty cannot be explained simply by lack of money; scarcity becomes the next object of suspicion.

signature_moments.moment: The bank that cannot collect from a king creates a new enemy to kill the king.
signature_moments.source_phrase: establish a new enemy to kill the king
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Jiang makes finance political and violent, not a neutral technical system.

signature_moments.moment: Power is the conversion of nothing into everything.
signature_moments.source_phrase: turn nothing, money, the contract, into everything
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This phrase becomes the core model that the whole lecture later names alchemy.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A crisis where more receipt-holders demand gold than the bank actually has.
Jiang's name for the evolved cartel system by which banks pool power and control modern money.

uncertainty_notes.note: The student phrase is truncated; treat it as a partial scarcity response rather than a clean public question.
